fields.Name: Common Threads Summit 2026: The Next Horizon

fields.Title: Keynote speaker: Treaty, rights and representation
fields.Bio: Ngarra Murray is a proud Wamba Wamba, Yorta Yorta, Dja Dja Wurrung and Dhudhuroa woman. She is a respected Aboriginal leader, treaty practitioner, and advocate for Indigenous self-determination. Ngarra is a former Co-Chair and inaugural member of the First Peoples' Assembly of Victoria, serving two terms and playing a central leadership role in guiding Victoria through Australia's first modern treaty-making process. As a signatory to the first modern treaty in Australia, Ngarra provided steady, principled leadership during a defining period in Victoria's history - helping shape the structures, frameworks, and relationships required to progress treaty negotiations between First Peoples and the State, and contributing to a foundation for future agreements across the continent. Beyond Victoria, Ngarra contributes to international Indigenous engagement, recognising the long-standing relationships and shared experiences of Indigenous peoples globally. Her work reflects a deep commitment to Indigenous-led diplomacy, solidarity between nations, and strengthening First Peoples' influence in local, national and international forums.
fields.Description: Keynote speaker for the Day 2 morning plenary on Treaty, rights and representation.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Keynote speaker: Climate justice and protecting Country
fields.Bio: Professor Anne Poelina PhD, PhD, MEd, MPH&TM, MA is a Nyikina Warrwa woman from the Kimberley region of Western Australia. An active community leader, human and earth rights advocate, poet and film maker. Chair Indigenous Knowledges and Senior Research Fellow Nulungu Institute Research University of Notre Dame, Adjunct Professor, College of Indigenous Education Futures, Arts & Society, Charles Darwin University, Darwin. Adjunct Professor School of Social Sciences, University of Western Australia, Visiting Fellow Crawford School of Public Policy at the Australian National University, Canberra. Elected Chair of the Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council (2018).
fields.Description: Keynote speaker for the Day 2 afternoon plenary on climate justice and protecting Country.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Keynote speaker: the moment we are in
fields.Bio: Celeste is an Arrernte women, a union organiser, a writer, an activist and a social commentator. Over decades now, she has been involved in multiple movements, and has additionally used her skills of discernment and relatability, to bring debates and observation to the media.
fields.Description: Keynote speaker for the Day 1 opening plenary.
Venue Name: Intercontinental Hotel, North Terrace, Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Leeroy Bilney is a proud Barngala, Kuprun, Wirangu, Kokatha, Mirning and Noongar man from Ceduna, South Australia. A nationally recognised leader across Indigenous philanthropy, governance, and community development, Leeroy brings deep experience spanning Aboriginal community-controlled organisations, government, and cross-sector reform. His work is grounded in strengthening Indigenous leadership, building community capacity, and driving systemic change. He is the Regional Manager (Australia) for the International Funders for Indigenous Peoples and Co-Founder and Director of the Spinifex Foundation SA. As the inaugural Co-Presiding Member of South Australia's First Nations Voice to Parliament, Leeroy continues to shape policy and advocacy at the highest levels. Drawing on storytelling and lived experience, he engages audiences to think differently, act with purpose, and invest in Indigenous-led futures.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 morning plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Kuruma Marthedunera woman Josie Alec is the First Nations Lead for Australian Conservation Foundation, the co-founder of Save our Songlines. "I have been blessed with an extraordinary life journey. From stolen generation, to growing up with a wonderful foster family, to making my way back home to my amazing family and culture." Spiritualism runs strong in both of my families and was the grounding I needed upon my return home. My Mum was a traditional healer and held many sacred gifts and knowledge about our country. Through that knowledge I was taught about our native ecology and the traditional use of native plants, their purpose and the connection to everything around them. Our Lore and Culture has played a big part in the rejuvenation and longevity for centuries in my country and informs the way we connect and live our daily lives. I became a teacher in 2013 and gained many skills within the community I lived in and bought my children up back on their country. I co-founded Save our Songlines which is a campaign we started to make people aware of the risk to our country and the sacred rock art that has been there for over 60,000 years. My niece and I went to Geneva to speak on behalf of our country and cultural practises at the United Nations First Nations conference in 2022. As the First nations Lead at ACF we created a documentary called Heart of Country, which explores different First Nations people across Australia and their plight to save their country. I am pleased to share my journey about learning how important our connection with country is and the care for country practices, as first nations people we share. Looking after the environment is key to our future.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 afternoon plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Karina Lester is a Yankunytjatjara Woman from the Anangu Pitjantjatjara Yankunytjatjara Lands (APY Lands), in far north west South Australia. Karina is the Co-Manager and Senior Aboriginal Language Expert for the Mobile Language Team and is a speaker, interpreter and translator of her Yankunytjatjara Wangka (language). Karina is also a second-generation survivor of the British Nuclear Tests at Emu Field in 1953 and the daughter of Anti-Nuc activist Yami Lester. She is also an Ambassador for the International Campaign to Abolish Nuclear Weapons (ICAN Australia) speaking strong about the harms felt by her people.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 afternoon plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Professor Jackie Huggins AM FAHA is Bidjara and BirriGubba Juru. She is currently Director of Partnerships and Innovation, Faculty of Health, Medicine and Behavioural Sciences, University of Queensland, Herston campus, Brisbane. Elder in Residence, Australian Broadcasting Commission Elder in Residence, Australian Progress and and Common Threads Co-Chair, National Apology Foundation Professor Huggins is in demand as a speaker, mentor, writer, advisor with over four decades experience in community, academia, government and non government sectors.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 morning plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Opening remarks
fields.Description: Delivered opening remarks in the Day 1 introduction to Common Threads.
Venue Name: Intercontinental Hotel, North Terrace, Adelaide

fields.Title: Panel discussion contributor
fields.Bio: Mililma May is a Danggalaba Kulumbringin Tiwi Rak Thanggural woman from Gulumoerrgin (so-called Darwin) in the Northern Territory, Australia. She is a writer and community organiser working across justice, movement building, and storytelling. She is the Executive Officer of Movement Building & Advocacy at Change the Record, and the former CEO and Co-Founder of Uprising of the People. Mililma has supported grassroots movements across the Northern Territory, including Shut Down Don Dale, Save Lee Point, and Stop Middle Arm. Her work is grounded in political analysis, deep historical awareness, and the authority of Aboriginal law and governance. Her writing has appeared in Cheek Media, Crikey, NITV, Kindling + Sage, and We Are Explorers.
fields.Description: Panel discussion contributor in the Day 1 afternoon plenary on frontline movements for justice.
Venue Name: Intercontinental Hotel, North Terrace, Adelaide

fields.Title: International guest speaker
fields.Bio: Taily Terena is an indigenous woman from the Terena Nation, from the Pantanal, the wetlands of Brazil. A land defender and climate advocate, she has been working for more than 15 years for the rights of Indigenous Peoples, focusing on indigenous women and youth. As an anthropologist, she has studied the history of her people through intergenerational knowledge and decolonization. Part of the Terena youth collective, Inamati Xane, she currently works with topics related to agroecology, climate change, and territory. She works at the IITC - International Indian Treaty Council in the Environmental Health and Food Sovereignty programme.
fields.Description: International guest speaker in the Day 2 morning plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Title: Lightning talk contributor
fields.Bio: Duane is a Wulgurukaba and Bidjara man, raised between Tennant Creek and Magnetic Island. For more than a decade his work has been rooted in Country, the Great Barrier Reef and the community-based programs that care for it, and grounded in a singular conviction: Country and People are one, and Traditional Owners must hold the power and capacity to safeguard their biocultural land and seascapes, their heritage, and their cultural expression. That work has taken him across senior statutory and community boards spanning regional development, marine and environmental science, environmental regulation, and tourism. He currently serves as CEO of the Council of First Nations and Co-Chair of ReefTO Taskforce. When mob stand shoulder to shoulder, organise, and prosecute our priorities collectively, we speak from a position of power, not permission. Unity is not a slogan. It is the foundation for the next chapter.
fields.Description: Lightning talk contributor in the Day 2 afternoon plenary block.
Venue Name: Adelaide Oval, War Memorial Drive, North Adelaide

fields.Name: Intercontinental Hotel
fields.Subtitle: Youth Summit Day + First Official Summit Day
fields.Description: Summit sessions at the Intercontinental Hotel run on Monday 11 May and Tuesday 12 May.
fields.Instructions:
The summit conference space is inside the hotel. Go to the lobby and follow signage to the Lower Lobby.

Navigating the venue:
- Ballroom: registration desk, plenary space
- Banksia and Grevillea rooms: breakout spaces
- Foyer area: catering space

If you require a quiet space, or a space to have a break with your children, ask a Common Threads team member for support.

Parking (Intercontinental): limited street parking nearby. Paid parking is available at Adelaide Convention Centre car parks (North Terrace, Riverbank, Festival). See parking details in the Details section for rates and locations.

Drop-off and pick-up: available adjacent to the hotel entrance with ramp access (taxis, rideshare, private cars).

Accessibility: wheelchair-accessible parking is available in the hotel car park and at Adelaide Convention Centre car parks.

fields.Name: Adelaide Oval
fields.Subtitle: Second Summit Day + Last Summit Day
fields.Address: War Memorial Dr, North Adelaide SA 5006, Australia.
fields.Description: Summit sessions at Adelaide Oval run on Wednesday 13 May and Thursday 14 May.
fields.Instructions:
Summit sessions are in the Ian McLachlan Room (Western Stand, Level 3). The nearest entry point is North Gate. If entering through South Gate, ushers will guide you. Guests entering via North Gate need to press the intercom button for stadium access.

Navigating the venue:
- Ian McLachlan Room: registration desk, main plenary space
- Ian McLachlan Room East: breakout space
- Ian McLachlan Room West: breakout space
- Village Green Pavilion: breakout space
- David Hookes Terrace + Village Green Pavilion: catering and outdoor space

If you require a quiet space, or a space to have a break with your children, ask a Common Threads team member for support.

Parking (Adelaide Oval): limited street parking nearby.
- Wilson Street Carpark: entry via King William Road. Use lifts to Level 1, enter via South Gate, then follow function room signage.
- Wilson North Carpark: entry via Pennington Terrace, North Adelaide. Enter via Bob Quinn Gate, then follow function room signage.

Function parking rate: $25 per day. Keep your entry ticket and collect a validation QR from the venue.

Access videos are referenced in summit comms for Wilson East Carpark and Oval Hotel guests.

fields.Title: Living Kaurna Cultural Centre, Warriparinga
fields.Description: A strong first stop. It is run through Southern Cultural Immersion, a 100% Aboriginal-owned organisation, at the culturally significant Warriparinga site near Marion. Good for Kaurna culture, events, education and walking tours.

fields.Title: Southern Cultural Immersion tours
fields.Description: A 100% Kaurna-owned and operated tourism business offering cultural walking tours, First Nations engagement and immersive experiences across the Adelaide Plains.

fields.Title: South Australian Museum, Australian Aboriginal Cultures Gallery
fields.Description: One of the major museum-based Aboriginal culture experiences in Adelaide, with more than 3,000 items across two floors. The Museum says it holds one of the world’s most comprehensive collections of Australian Aboriginal cultural material.

fields.Title: Kaurna Walking Trail
fields.Description: A city-based trail linking 17 significant Kaurna sites along Karrawirra Pari / River Torrens, North Terrace and Tarntanyangga / Victoria Square. Good if you want a self-guided city walk.

fields.Title: Adelaide Botanic Garden Cultural Garden Tours
fields.Description: Cultural tours focused on native plants and their importance to First Nations people. Good for learning about plant use, bush foods and medicines in a calm setting.

fields.Title: Kaurna Reconciliation Sculptures, Adelaide Festival Centre
fields.Description: Public artwork and cultural site on the Riverbank, connected to Kaurna reconciliation and place. Easy to combine with the Kaurna Walking Trail.

fields.Title: Glenthorne National Park-Ityamaiitpinna Yarta
fields.Description: A Kaurna-significant national park south of the city, with interpretive signage, carvings, sculptures and the “Walking with Winaityinaityi” trail.

fields.Title: Yitpi Yartapuultiku
fields.Description: A cultural place in the Port Adelaide Enfield area where Kaurna traditions, knowledge and contemporary innovation meet. Good for deeper local cultural learning if events or programs are running.

fields.Title: Tarnanthi / Art Gallery of South Australia
fields.Description: Tarnanthi is AGSA’s major festival and platform for contemporary Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander art. Worth checking current exhibitions before visiting, as programming changes.

fields.Name: Common Threads
fields.Summary: Common Threads is a First Nations-led Australian organisation that runs advocacy and campaign work on truth-telling, Treaty, land rights, and climate justice.

fields.Name: Deshong Consulting
fields.Summary: Deshong Consulting is an Indigenous-led consulting practice focused on governance, facilitation, leadership development, advocacy, and community capacity outcomes.

fields.Name: Australian Progress
fields.Summary: Australian Progress builds the advocacy, leadership, and campaign capacity of social change organisations and civil society movements in Australia.

fields.Name: SOSBlak Australia SA
fields.Summary: SOS Blak Australia is a grassroots Aboriginal rights campaign best known for mobilising against forced closure of remote Aboriginal communities.

fields.Name: No Underground Coal Gasification at Leigh Creek
fields.Summary: This campaign supports Adnyamathanha community opposition to underground coal gasification near Leigh Creek and Copley.

fields.Name: Pormpuraaw Aboriginal Shire Council
fields.Summary: Pormpuraaw Aboriginal Shire Council is the local government authority delivering civic services and community development in Pormpuraaw.

fields.Name: Lake Tyers Aboriginal Trust
fields.Summary: Lake Tyers Aboriginal Trust is an Aboriginal-governed body that manages trust land, governance, and community matters at Lake Tyers.

fields.Name: University of Adelaide
fields.Summary: The University of Adelaide is a major South Australian university institution focused on higher education and research.

fields.Name: Mandingalby Yidinji Aboriginal Corporation
fields.Summary: Mandingalbay Yidinji Aboriginal Corporation is the native title representative body for Mandingalbay Yidinji people and their country near Cairns.

fields.Name: Nanum Wungthim Land & Sea Management Group
fields.Summary: Nanum Wungthim is an Indigenous ranger group in Napranum that delivers coastal, cultural heritage, and land-and-sea management work.

fields.Name: Lachlan Kalari River Aboriginal Water Council
fields.Summary: Lachlan Kalari River Aboriginal Water Council is an Aboriginal water governance and advocacy body associated with the Lachlan (Kalari) river region.

fields.Name: Government Architect NSW
fields.Summary: Government Architect NSW provides strategic design leadership to improve architecture, urban design, and landscape outcomes across NSW.

fields.Name: Full Stop Australia
fields.Summary: Full Stop Australia is a national specialist service providing trauma-informed support and 24/7 counselling related to sexual, domestic, and family violence.

fields.Name: Change the Record
fields.Summary: Change the Record is a First Nations-led coalition campaigning to end over-incarceration and systemic violence against Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.

fields.Name: Wenlock Catchment Management Group Inc
fields.Summary: Wenlock Catchment Management Group Inc is a Cape York catchment group focused on river and landscape stewardship in the Wenlock region.

fields.Name: Mounty Aboriginal Youth and Community Service
fields.Summary: Mounty Aboriginal Youth and Community Service is an Aboriginal-controlled Mt Druitt youth service supporting community-led programs and justice reinvestment outcomes.

fields.Name: Western Sydney Watch Committee
fields.Summary: The Watch Committee monitors Aboriginal treatment in custody and advocates on deaths in custody, policing, and justice-system accountability.

fields.Name: Our Islands Our Home
fields.Summary: Our Islands Our Home is a Torres Strait Islander-led climate justice campaign seeking action to protect Zenadth Kes from climate impacts.

fields.Name: Mehi Centre
fields.Summary: Mehi Centre is a not-for-profit based in Moree that supports Indigenous communities through cultural, educational, environmental, and advocacy initiatives.

fields.Name: Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council
fields.Summary: Martuwarra Fitzroy River Council is an Indigenous-led alliance of Kimberley Elders advocating to protect the Martuwarra/Fitzroy River system.

fields.Name: National Tertiary Education Union
fields.Summary: The National Tertiary Education Union is the main union representing university and tertiary education workers across Australia.

fields.Name: Fisher's Oysters Pty Ltd
fields.Summary: Fishers of Freycinet is a Tasmania-based seafood and oyster business associated with the Fisher family’s farmed oyster operations.

fields.Name: First Peoples' Assembly of Victoria
fields.Summary: The First Peoples' Assembly of Victoria is an independent, democratically elected body representing Traditional Owners and First Peoples in Victoria in Treaty-related processes.

fields.Name: Australian Conservation Foundation
fields.Summary: The Australian Conservation Foundation is a national environmental advocacy organisation campaigning to protect nature and address climate pollution.

fields.Name: Limestone Coast Landscape Board
fields.Summary: The Limestone Coast Landscape Board is a South Australian statutory board that leads regional landscape and natural resource management.

fields.Name: Strait Climate Action
fields.Summary: Strait Climate Action is a Torres Strait youth climate group creating language-based resources and intergenerational climate campaigning.

fields.Name: North Queensland Land Council
fields.Summary: The North Queensland Land Council is the recognised Native Title Representative Body supporting Traditional Owners across its North Queensland region.

fields.Name: Wardliparingga - SAHMRI
fields.Summary: Wardliparingga is SAHMRI’s Aboriginal Health Equity research theme focused on improving health and wellbeing outcomes for Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander peoples.

fields.Name: Tribes United
fields.Summary: Spiritual Unity of the Tribes is an Australian intertribal community focused on ceremony, cultural continuity, and traditional knowledge sharing.

fields.Name: Social Reinvestment WA
fields.Summary: Social Reinvestment WA is an Aboriginal-led coalition advocating justice reinvestment and reforms to reduce Aboriginal over-representation in WA’s justice system.

fields.Name: Human Rights Law Centre
fields.Summary: The Human Rights Law Centre is a legal advocacy organisation that works to advance and defend human rights through law reform, litigation, and public campaigning.

fields.Name: Queensland Teachers' Union
fields.Summary: The Queensland Teachers' Union is the industrial and professional union representing educators in Queensland state schools and TAFE.

fields.Name: Tasmanian Aboriginal Centre
fields.Summary: The Tasmanian Aboriginal Centre is an Aboriginal community organisation delivering statewide advocacy, health, cultural, language, and community programs.

fields.Name: ARDS Aboriginal Corporation
fields.Summary: ARDS Aboriginal Corporation is a Yolŋu-led organisation that supports language, culture, education, and community development in Northeast Arnhem Land.

fields.Name: The Nurse On Foot
fields.Summary: The Foot Care Nurse is a nurse-led mobile and clinic foot-care service providing in-home nail and foot health support in Victoria.

fields.Name: Mob Made Media & Communications Agency
fields.Summary: Mob Made Media & Communications Agency is a First Nations media and content production agency credited on arts and community campaign work.

fields.Name: First Australians Capital
fields.Summary: First Australians Capital is a First Nations-led impact investment and business support organisation backing Indigenous entrepreneurs and enterprises.

fields.Name: Invasive Species Council
fields.Summary: The Invasive Species Council is an Australian environmental advocacy organisation working to prevent and reduce harm from invasive species.

fields.Name: Pitta Pitta Aboriginal Corporation RNTBC
fields.Summary: Pitta Pitta Aboriginal Corporation RNTBC acts as trustee for the native title rights and interests of the Pitta Pitta People.

fields.Name: Aboriginal Housing Office
fields.Summary: The Aboriginal Housing Office is a NSW statutory body responsible for improving access to affordable housing for Aboriginal people.

fields.Name: Katji Yanun
fields.Summary: Katji Yanun is an Aboriginal-led consulting practice focused on justice, governance, and systems reform advisory work.

fields.Name: Justice Reinvestment Port Augusta
fields.Summary: Justice Reinvestment Port Augusta is a community-led Port Augusta initiative linked to Aboriginal-led justice reinvestment work delivered via Healthy Dreaming.

fields.Name: Justice for Walker
fields.Summary: Justice for Walker is a grassroots campaign calling for accountability and justice after the death of Warlpiri man Kumanjayi Walker.

fields.Name: Pukatja Community
fields.Summary: Pukatja Community is an Anangu community on the APY Lands in north-west South Australia.

fields.Name: National Disability Insurance Scheme
fields.Summary: The National Disability Insurance Scheme is Australia's national program that funds supports for eligible people with significant and permanent disability.

fields.Name: Seed Mob
fields.Summary: Seed Mob is an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander youth-led climate justice movement focused on protecting Country and communities.

fields.Name: Pacific Australian Emerging Leaders Network
fields.Summary: The Pacific Australian Emerging Leaders Network connects young leaders across the Pacific and Australia for leadership, advocacy, and regional partnership.

fields.Name: Murrie Connections
fields.Summary: Murrie Connections is an Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islander-owned organisation that recruits culturally appropriate participants for research and consulting projects.

fields.Name: Mapoon Aboriginal Shire Council
fields.Summary: Mapoon Aboriginal Shire Council is the local government authority serving the Mapoon community in Cape York, Queensland.

fields.Name: Deadly Inspiring Youth Doing Good ATSI Corporation
fields.Summary: Deadly Inspiring Youth Doing Good ATSI Corporation is a youth-led First Nations not-for-profit that empowers young people through leadership and community programs.

fields.Name: Aboriginal Community Connect
fields.Summary: Aboriginal Community Connect is a culturally focused support program helping Aboriginal people address alcohol and other drug issues while staying connected to culture and community.

fields.Name: Thriving Families SA
fields.Summary: Thriving Families SA is a South Australian cross-sector initiative focused on prevention and early supports to improve outcomes for children, families, and communities.

fields.Name: Brisbane Blacks
fields.Summary: Brisbane Blacks is a grassroots Aboriginal-led collective associated with community advocacy and political organising in Brisbane.

fields.Name: End Toxic Prisons Campaign
fields.Summary: End Toxic Prisons Campaign is an abolitionist campaign opposing youth incarceration and advocating community-controlled alternatives, especially for First Nations young people.

fields.Name: First Nations Futures
fields.Summary: First Nations Futures is a First Nations-led economic justice organisation that redistributes flexible funding to First Nations community initiatives.

fields.Name: Woven Project
fields.Summary: Woven Project is an Indigenous-led climate justice initiative that builds global Indigenous collaboration, strategy, and advocacy for land rights and climate action.

fields.Name: Charles Campbell College
fields.Summary: Charles Campbell College is a South Australian government Reception-to-Year-12 school in Adelaide.

fields.Name: Digital Storytellers
fields.Summary: Digital Storytellers is an Australian social impact storytelling agency that helps purpose-driven organisations create narrative-led campaigns and media.

fields.Name: Gomeroi Traditional Owners Aboriginal Corporation
fields.Summary: This corporation entry represents an Aboriginal organisation connected to Gomeroi/Bigambul traditional owners and family groups.

fields.Name: Bama Connect Consultant
fields.Summary: Bama Connect Consultant provides culturally grounded consulting in Traditional Owner engagement, governance transition support, and on-Country facilitation.

fields.Name: CFN
fields.Summary: CFN is a Queensland not-for-profit disability support provider delivering community participation, respite, and daily living services.

fields.Name: Apunipima Cape York Health Council
fields.Summary: Apunipima Cape York Health Council is an Aboriginal Community Controlled Health Organisation delivering comprehensive primary health care across Cape York.

fields.Name: Australian Education Union
fields.Summary: The Australian Education Union is a national union representing educators in public early childhood, school, and TAFE settings.

fields.Name: Charles Sturt University
fields.Summary: Charles Sturt University is an Australian public university with major regional campuses and a strong focus on applied education and research.

fields.Name: Children's Ground
fields.Summary: Children's Ground is a First Nations-led organisation delivering long-term community-led systems change so First Nations children can thrive in culture and identity.

fields.Name: Climate Action Network Australia

fields.Name: Common Threads Community
fields.Description: Community supporters helping make Summit 2026 possible.

fields.Name: Summit session feedback
fields.Description: Share feedback on sessions, logistics and support.

fields.Subtitle: How We Show Up In The Space
fields.Content Body:
We are committed to creating a safe and inclusive experience at Common Threads for everyone, regardless of your gender, gender identity, sexual orientation, ethnicity, nationality, disability, physical appearance, body size, race, age, religion, economic status, immigration status or refugee status. We do not tolerate harassment of participants, staff or volunteers in any form, and any person who violates these rules may be removed from the event without a refund.

With over 300 First Nations campaigners, community organisers, communicators and changemakers coming together from all corners of the country, there is huge strength in our shared values and collective vision for the future, as well as strength in the diversity of our opinions, perspectives, experiences and approaches to creating change.

There is a responsibility on all of us to help create such a space. As such, we've developed a set of principles for how we show up in the space, alongside a code of conduct.
Please read principles and full code of conduct here.

If someone makes you or anyone else feel unsafe or unwelcome, please report it as soon as possible to our wellbeing and grievance coordinators x and x for a confidential conversation (contact details below).

Use strong passwords and multi-factor authentication on devices used during summit days.

Do not share personal or sensitive details in public channels.

Report suspicious digital activity to summit crew straight away.
